officialSources.title: Extend Google Sheets
evidence: Google documents Apps Script APIs for programmatically creating, reading, and editing Sheets.
Google documents bound scripts with Sheets UI integration and automatic responses when the spreadsheet is opened.

officialSources.title: Custom Functions in Google Sheets
evidence: Google documents Apps Script custom functions that can be used from Sheets like built-in functions.
Google documents custom-function recalculation, side-effect, deterministic-argument, and runtime constraints.

officialSources.title: Google Sheets Macros
evidence: Google documents recording Sheets macros that generate bound Apps Script functions.
Google documents that macros can be edited in Apps Script or written from scratch and imported into Sheets.

officialSources.title: Simple Triggers
evidence: Google documents Sheets-compatible triggers such as open, edit, and selection-change handlers.
Google documents trigger queueing and skipped selection-change behavior under rapid changes.

officialSources.title: Quotas for Google Services
evidence: Google documents Apps Script quotas and limitations for script runtime, custom-function runtime, simultaneous executions, and trigger runtime.
Google documents quota and limitation exception messages for service calls, computer time, and repeated custom-function execution.

officialSources.title: Office Scripts in Excel
evidence: Microsoft documents Office Scripts for automating day-to-day Excel tasks with the action recorder and code editor.
Microsoft documents running scripts from workbook buttons and using Power Automate for broader workflows.

officialSources.title: Fundamentals for Office Scripts in Excel
evidence: Microsoft documents a typed ExcelScript.Workbook object passed into the script main function.
Microsoft documents object-model access for worksheets, tables, charts, ranges, comments, PivotTables, shapes, and related Excel objects.

officialSources.title: Platform limits, requirements, and error messages for Office Scripts
evidence: Microsoft documents Office Scripts platform limits, requirements, and error handling.
Microsoft documents that external fetch calls fail when an Office Script runs through Power Automate and need equivalent connector actions.

officialSources.title: Differences between Office Scripts and VBA macros
evidence: Microsoft documents Office Scripts as secure, cross-platform, cloud-based automation compared with desktop-centric VBA.
Microsoft documents Office Scripts workbook-only access, administrator controls, licensing considerations, and platform support differences.

officialSources.title: Run Office Scripts with Power Automate
evidence: Microsoft documents adding Office Scripts to Power Automate workflows across workbook and non-workbook services.
Microsoft documents passing static values, expressions, and dynamic content between Power Automate flows and scripts.

limitations: This artifact captures current official public documentation, not a live tenant execution benchmark against Google Apps Script or Microsoft Office Scripts.
The comparison proves direct external automation evidence exists; it does not claim bilig is 10x better across automation or every objective category.

dimensions.title: Automation Surface And Workflow
biligEvidence: The automation scorecard executes semantic workbook-agent command bundle validation, preview projection, apply behavior, worker-runtime preview, and headless WorkPaper workflows.
The workbook-agent registry exposes canonical typed tools for semantic workbook inspection, mutation, preview, undo, object, protection, and media operations.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google documents Apps Script APIs for Sheets automation, bound scripts, macros, custom menus, and event triggers.
Google documents Sheets macro recording into editable Apps Script functions.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft documents Office Scripts action recording, script editing, workbook buttons, sharing, and Power Automate integration.
Microsoft documents the ExcelScript.Workbook object as the top-level script interaction surface.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig has repo-executable typed semantic command and preview/apply controls; Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel have documented script frameworks centered on Apps Script and Office Scripts.
residualLimitations: bilig still needs live incumbent execution benchmarks before claiming automation dominance over Google Apps Script or Office Scripts.
Official docs can lag tenant-specific feature rollout, admin policy, and license configuration.

dimensions.title: Formula Custom Function Integration
biligEvidence: bilig's automation evidence focuses on workbook commands that can write formulas, set ranges, format cells, and preview the resulting formula effects before apply.
bilig's broader formula and dominance artifacts track runtime formula production coverage separately from workbook automation controls.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google documents Apps Script custom functions callable from Sheets formulas and subject to custom-function recalculation and runtime limits.
Google documents that custom functions return values and cannot arbitrarily edit cells outside their returned output.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft Office Scripts automate Excel through scripts, recorders, buttons, and Power Automate rather than by adding worksheet custom functions.
Microsoft documents Office Scripts separately from desktop VBA macros and Office Add-ins.
dimensions.comparisonResult: Google Sheets has a documented custom-function formula extension path; bilig currently has stronger checked preview/apply command semantics but not a matching custom-function plugin surface, and Office Scripts is positioned as task automation rather than formula UDF extension.
residualLimitations: bilig needs a first-class custom function or add-in API artifact before it can claim complete automation/API superiority in formula extensibility.
This dimension records evidence and gaps instead of converting the category into a blanket win.

dimensions.title: Execution Limits And Runtime Governance
biligEvidence: The automation scorecard's semantic workflow benchmark measures host-call reduction by comparing five typed semantic commands against 1202 incumbent-style row-by-row script-visible operations.
The agent execution policy and auditability scorecards separately verify permission and preview/apply rails for agent-driven workbook mutation.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google documents Apps Script script-runtime, custom-function-runtime, simultaneous-execution, trigger-count, and daily trigger-runtime limits.
Google documents trigger queueing and quota exception modes that automation code must handle.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft documents Office Scripts platform limits, supported environments, Power Automate restrictions, and external-call behavior.
Microsoft documents workbook-only script access, tenant-level controls, and sharing controls for Office Scripts.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig can prove a 10x host-call reduction for one local semantic workflow, while Google Apps Script and Office Scripts publish runtime and governance limits that must be handled by script authors.
residualLimitations: The 10x workflow result is workload-specific and local; it is not a live Google or Microsoft runtime timing comparison.
bilig still needs production runtime quotas, cancellation, and tenant-policy artifacts before the automation governance claim is complete.

dimensions.title: Typed API And Local Automation
biligEvidence: bilig validates command bundles with typed semantic workbook commands, affected ranges, preview summaries, and deterministic application into the SpreadsheetEngine.
bilig verifies headless WorkPaper automation through copy, paste, batch formula writes, persistence round trip, undo, and redo without browser DOM automation.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google Apps Script uses JavaScript and spreadsheet-bound APIs to automate Sheets and expose custom functions.
Google Sheets custom functions and triggers are constrained by documented recalculation, side-effect, and runtime limits.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft Office Scripts use TypeScript-facing ExcelScript object types with a workbook root object.
Microsoft positions Office Scripts as cloud-based cross-platform task automation with workbook-scoped access.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig's checked automation model is local-first, previewable, undoable, and expressed as typed workbook semantics; Google and Microsoft expose mature script ecosystems with documented cloud or workspace constraints.
residualLimitations: bilig needs public SDK documentation and external-client smoke tests before this becomes an API ecosystem lead claim.
The comparison intentionally separates bilig's repo-executable semantics from incumbent marketplace, add-on, and enterprise ecosystem breadth.
